naturalMeaning: A casual after-work drinking-and-eating spot — small shared plates, cheap drinks, loud and informal, closer to a tapas bar than a restaurant.

examples.text: 今日は居酒屋に行きませんか。
examples.reading: きょうはいざかやにいきませんか。
examples.romaji: Kyō wa izakaya ni ikimasen ka.
examples.translation: Want to go to an izakaya today?

naturalMeaning: A fixed-price, time-limited drink plan — typically 90 or 120 minutes — ordered for the whole table at once, not per person on request.

examples.text: 飲み放題は九十分です。
examples.reading: のみほうだいはきゅうじゅっぷんです。

naturalMeaning: The reflexive first order at almost any izakaya gathering — you order beer for the whole table before anyone has even opened the menu, then figure out food and other drinks after. See the culture notes.

examples.text: とりあえずビールでいいですか。
examples.romaji: Toriaezu bīru de ii desu ka.
examples.translation: Beer to start for everyone, is that okay?

naturalMeaning: Often shortened to just 「生」 (nama) when ordering — "nama hitotsu" is enough to get a draft beer.
Shochu mixed with soda and usually flavored syrup or juice — lighter and sweeter than it sounds, a common alternative to beer.
The single most popular izakaya chūhai flavor — shochu, soda, and lemon, often served with a wedge to squeeze in yourself.
The formal/generic term — the loanword "sake" (酒) in Japanese can just mean "alcohol" in general, so 日本酒 is the word to actually order rice wine.

examples.text: 日本酒は熱燗と冷やがあります。
examples.reading: にほんしゅはあつかんとひやがあります。
examples.romaji: Nihonshu wa atsukan to hiya ga arimasu.
examples.translation: For sake, we have it warmed or chilled.

naturalMeaning: Distilled, usually from sweet potato, barley, or rice — stronger than sake, often drunk on the rocks, with water (水割り), or as a highball base.
Shochu mixed with oolong tea instead of soda — a common lower-sugar order for people who don't want a sweet chūhai.
A tiny dish brought out automatically the moment you sit down — you didn't order it, and it isn't free. Functions as a seating/table charge disguised as food. See the culture notes.

examples.text: お通し代は三百円です。
examples.reading: おとおしだいはさんびゃくえんです。

translation: small starter dish (same idea as otōshi)
naturalMeaning: Largely interchangeable with お通し — つきだし is heard more in the Kansai region, おとおし more in and around Tokyo.
Said together, glasses raised, before anyone takes their first sip — drinking before the group toast is considered a small faux pas.

examples.text: すみません、もう一杯ください。
examples.reading: すみません、もういっぱいください。

reading: おなじものをもうひとつ
translation: another one of the same, please

naturalMeaning: Ordered by the cut, not just "chicken" — a table usually orders a mixed assortment across several cuts rather than many of one kind.

examples.text: 焼き鳥の盛り合わせをお願いします。
examples.reading: やきとりのもりあわせをおねがいします。

naturalMeaning: The most common, crowd-pleasing yakitori cut — juicy dark meat, a safe default order.
Often served with a raw egg yolk on the side for dipping — ask for 卵付き (tamago-tsuki) if you want it that way.
Grilled until crisp — one of the more polarizing cuts for first-timers, but a favorite among regulars.
Prized for its crunchy texture rather than flavor — a very different eating experience from the other cuts.
One of the two standard ways to season yakitori — a thick, sweet-savory brushed sauce, as opposed to plain salt.

examples.text: ねぎまは塩でお願いします。
examples.reading: ねぎまはしおでおねがいします。

naturalMeaning: The default order while everyone's still deciding on the rest of the meal — cheap, fast, and eaten straight from the pod.
Bite-sized, marinated in soy/garlic/ginger before frying — a near-universal izakaya order, distinct from Western fried chicken.

translation: cold tofu (topped with green onion and grated ginger)

naturalMeaning: Lightly dusted with starch, fried, then served in a savory dashi-based sauce — different from hiyayakko, which is served cold and plain.
Raised, shoe-off floor seating, often behind a sliding partition for a semi-private feel — the classic izakaya seating style alongside counter and table seats.
Required before stepping up onto a 座敷 — shoes are usually left in a small cubby or on a step just below the raised floor.
Seating right at the bar facing the kitchen or grill — common for solo diners and couples, and often first-come first-served.
A fully enclosed room, sometimes reservation-only — common for larger groups or work parties (nomikai) who want privacy.
Stretched out and said at some volume across the room — the normal, non-rude way to summon staff at an izakaya, since nobody comes to check on you unprompted. See the culture notes.

reading: ちゅうもんをおねがいします

naturalMeaning: Older or more blunt-sounding than お会計 — お勘定 with the honorific prefix softens it back to polite register.

reading: おかいけいおねがいします

naturalMeaning: The default way groups pay in Japan — the total is usually divided equally per head rather than itemized per person, even if orders weren't identical. See the culture notes.

examples.text: 今日は割り勘にしましょう。
examples.reading: きょうはわりかんにしましょう。
examples.translation: Let's split the bill evenly today.

examples.text: お会計は別々にできますか。
examples.reading: おかいけいはべつべつにできますか。
examples.romaji: Okaikei wa betsubetsu ni dekimasu ka.

reading: いっしょにしてください
translation: Please put it all on one bill.

reading: のみほうだいのじかん
naturalMeaning: Usually announced or reminded near the end — staff will often check in with 'あと何分です' (there are X minutes left) shortly before it expires.
The final call for ordering, both for a nomihōdai window and for the restaurant's closing time — worth listening for so you're not caught wanting one more round.
